does: This paper presents a multi-agent visual-language framework for highway scene understanding that combines a large generic VLM (GPT-4o) with a smaller efficient VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) through a mixture-of-experts approach. The large model generates task-specific chain-of-thought prompts that guide the smaller model in reasoning over video sequences to perform multiple simultaneous perception tasks: weather classification, pavement wetness assessment, and traffic congestion detection. The system leverages complementary modalities including video and road weather sensor data, with experimental validation on three curated specialized datasets, including a novel multimodal pavement wetness dataset combining video with sensor information.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in vision-language models and their application to visual reasoning tasks, extending prior research on using large models for prompt engineering and knowledge distillation. It responds to the growing interest in efficient multi-task learning systems that balance accuracy with computational constraints by combining large foundation models with smaller task-specific variants. The mixture-of-experts strategy draws from established ensemble methods in machine learning, while the chain-of-thought prompting approach builds on recent work demonstrating improved reasoning in VLMs through structured prompt design. The focus on autonomous driving perception connects to ongoing research in scene understanding for autonomous vehicles and highway safety monitoring.
enables: This framework enables future development of efficient, real-time highway monitoring systems deployable on edge devices with computational constraints. The multimodal approach combining video with sensor data opens pathways for richer scene understanding by integrating diverse information sources. The methodology could extend to additional highway perception tasks beyond the three demonstrated, supporting comprehensive autonomous vehicle pipelines. The curated multimodal datasets provide resources for future research in weather-aware and road-condition prediction. The demonstrated strategy of using large models to guide smaller ones offers a template for deploying expensive foundation models in practical applications while maintaining efficiency, potentially transferring to other visual understanding domains requiring both accuracy and computational feasibility.
